nodes.title: WIP: fix(gnovm): tuple assignment — desugar multi-assign to per-slot stores
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"**WIP — alternative implementation for comparison. Do not merge.** Companion to #5765 (the currently-proposed fix) and the in-place variant (#5790). All three are off `master` and pass the identical correctness contract; this PR exists to compare implementation cost.

## Bug
Same as #5765 / golang/go#23017 + duplicate-LHS left-to-right ordering.

## Approach: per-slot store scheduling (op-level desugar)
Mirrors how Go's compiler lowers `OAS2` into a sequence of single stores — but done at the op layer, not as an AST/preprocess rewrite (the AST route was rejected: lowering map/`*p` LHS to address-temps would move slot-creation/deref out of store order and fight the #23017 semantics).

- `doOpAssign` no longer contains a multi-LHS resolve-then-store loop. For `n>1` it re-arranges the already-evaluated stack into one self-contained group per slot and schedules a new `OpAssignSlot` op per slot (slot-0 on top, runs first via LIFO).
- `doOpAssignSlot` resolves one LHS pointer (`PopAsPointer`, may panic) and stores it — an independent step. A panic in slot *i* runs only after slots 0..i-1 fully committed, so **panic atomicity falls out structurally** rather than from a careful interleave.
- Single-target (`n==1`, Go's `OAS`) keeps a direct fast path.
- Adds `OpAssignSlot` (0x8F), its dispatch case, a hand-patched stringer entry, and `numStackValuesForPointer`.

## Trade-off vs #5790
More faithful to Go's lowering and structurally cleaner on panic-ordering, but larger surface (+1 op, +dispatch, +stringer, +doOp fn, value-stack-carried slot index) and **heavier allocation** (a `make` + per-slot snapshots), not lighter. Same observable behavior, same total gas charge.

## Tests
- `gnovm/tests/files/assign_tuple_order.gno`, `assign40.gno`, `assign41.gno` (shared contract; unit test omitted — it couples to the old runtime shape).
- Verified: `go build ./gnovm/...`, `TestFiles/assign` (short), `gno.land/pkg/sdk/vm -run Gas`, `gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run Files -test.short` — all green, no regressions.","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5791,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): evaluate LHS operands then assign left-to-right in tuple assignment
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"Subsumes #5720.

## Bug 1 — mid-statement panic atomicity (golang/go#23017)

Tuple assignment `m[k], *p = 42, 2` panicked on `*p` before `m[k]` was written. The old `doOpAssign` resolved each LHS pointer and assigned in one right-to-left pass, so any panic from `PopAsPointer` (nil-deref, OOB, nil-map) aborted the statement before earlier writes.

## Bug 2 — left-to-right assignment order for duplicate LHS

`a, a, a = 1, 2, 3` left `a == 1` instead of the spec-required `a == 3` ([Go spec §Assignments](https://go.dev/ref/spec#Assignments): "the assignments are carried out in left-to-right order"). Same root cause: the old loop ran right-to-left.

## Fix

`doOpAssign` now:
- Single-LHS fast path: no buffering — preserves the common case's hot path.
- Multi-LHS: buffer each LHS's operand frame (popped in reverse to match the value-stack LIFO from `PushForPointer`), then push-back / `PopAsPointer` / `Assign2` **interleaved** in left-to-right order. A mid-statement panic leaves `L_0..L_{i-1}` written; duplicate LHS targets observe left-to-right writes.

Added `numStackValuesForPointer` helper alongside `PushForPointer` / `PopAsPointer2` (must stay in sync with both).

## Test plan

- [x] `gnovm/tests/files/assign_tuple_order.gno` — `m[k], *p = 42, 2` survives panic, `m[2] == 42`.
- [x] `gnovm/tests/files/assign40.gno` — `a, a, a = 1, 2, 3` → `a == 3`.
- [x] `gnovm/tests/files/assign41.gno` — duplicate LHS across IndexExpr / MapIndex / StarExpr / SelectorExpr / blank-interleaved / multi-return RHS / mixed-type blanks.
- [x] `gnovm/pkg/gnolang/op_assign_test.go` — unit tests for NameExpr (n=2,3,5,17) and IndexExpr reverse-pop.
- [x] `go test ./gno.land/pkg/sdk/vm/ -run Gas`
- [x] `go test ./gno.land/pkg/integration/ -run txtar`
- [x] `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/ -run Files -test.short` (all `assign*` filetests including the new ones)","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5765,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: WIP: fix(gnovm): tuple assignment — resolve LHS in-place, assign left-to-right
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"**WIP — alternative implementation for comparison. Do not merge.** Companion to #5765 (the currently-proposed fix) and the desugar variant. All three are off `master` and pass the identical correctness contract; this PR exists to compare implementation cost.

## Bug
Same as #5765: tuple assignment resolved LHS pointers and stored in one right-to-left loop, so (1) `m[k], *p = 42, 2` panicked on `*p` before committing `m[k]` (golang/go#23017), and (2) `a, a, a = 1, 2, 3` left `a==1` instead of `3`.

## Approach: in-place slice read
- Extract `PopAsPointer2`'s body into a pure `resolvePointer(lx, ops []TypedValue)` that reads operand values from a passed slice instead of popping the value stack. `PopAsPointer2` becomes a thin wrapper (slice top N → resolve → truncate), so all ~15 other callers are byte-for-byte unaffected.
- `doOpAssign`: single-LHS fast path unchanged; multi-LHS takes ONE slice window over the LHS operand region of `m.Values`, truncates once, then resolves+assigns each LHS left-to-right interleaved (panic atomicity preserved).
- Adds `numStackValuesForPointer` helper (kept in sync with `PushForPointer`/`resolvePointer`).

## Why it's interesting
**Zero heap allocation on the multi-LHS path** (vs the +1 `make([][]TypedValue,N)` alloc in #5765), so it likely closes the ~20-30% per-op regression #5765 tracked in #5789 — without a gas recalibration. The `resolvePointer` extraction is also a standalone readability win.

## Tests
- `gnovm/tests/files/assign_tuple_order.gno`, `assign40.gno`, `assign41.gno` (shared contract)
- `gnovm/pkg/gnolang/op_assign_test.go` (unit tests, still apply)
- Verified: `go build ./gnovm/...`, `TestDoOpAssign|TestFiles/assign` (short), `gno.land/pkg/sdk/vm -run Gas`, `gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run Files -test.short` — all green.","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5790,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: gas: recalibrate OpCPUSlopeAssign for multi-LHS path after #5765
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":[],"body":"Follow-up to #5765 (fix for golang/go#23017 + duplicate-LHS left-to-right ordering).

That PR changed the multi-LHS path of \`doOpAssign\` from a single right-to-left resolve+assign loop to a buffered two-pass design: pop each LHS's operand frame into a local slice, then push-back / \`PopAsPointer\` / \`Assign2\` interleaved left-to-right. This is required for correctness (mid-statement panic must leave earlier assignments intact; duplicate LHS must observe left-to-right writes).

A dev-laptop triage bench (\`op_assign_bench_test.go\`, Apple M3) shows the **multi-LHS** path got materially slower; the **single-LHS fast path** is unchanged.

### Bench (median of 5×2s)

**NameExpr LHS:**

| N | before ns | after ns | Δ | allocs |
|---|-----------|----------|-----|--------|
| 1 | 40.4 | 40.2 | −0.5% | 0 → 0 |
| 2 | 76.4 | 98.1 | +28% | 0 → 1 (48 B) |
| 3 | 117.8 | 144.7 | +23% | 0 → 1 (80 B) |
| 5 | 189.2 | 225.3 | +19% | 0 → 1 (128 B) |

**IndexExpr LHS (worst case — 2 operand values per LHS):**

| N | before ns | after ns | Δ | allocs |
|---|-----------|----------|-----|--------|
| 1 | 53.4 | 52.8 | −1.2% | 0 → 0 |
| 2 | 109.4 | 143.0 | +31% | 0 → 1 (48 B) |
| 3 | 160.2 | 202.3 | +26% | 0 → 1 (80 B) |
| 5 | 263.8 | 317.5 | +20% | 0 → 1 (128 B) |

### Why this is a follow-up, not a blocker for #5765

- Numbers are dev-hardware deltas, not absolute. Setting the new \`OpCPUSlopeAssign\` (or a separate multi-LHS surcharge) requires the consensus reference hardware.
- The fix is for two correctness bugs; gas accounting can land separately.

### Scope

- Decide: bump \`OpCPUSlopeAssign\` globally, or add a per-call surcharge gated on \`len(s.Lhs) > 1\` (the latter avoids taxing the common single-LHS fast path).
- Run \`op_assign_bench_test.go\` (already in repo) on consensus hardware before and after the fix to set the constant.
- Account for the +1 heap alloc per multi-LHS call (24·N bytes for the \`[][]TypedValue\` backing).

### TODO marker

\`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/op_assign.go\` contains a \`// TODO(gas)\` comment pointing here next to the \`make([][]TypedValue, ...)\` line.","kind":"issue","labels":[],"number":5789,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): allow unsealed *DeclaredType during mutual type-decl recursion
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"## Summary

`tryPredefine` panicked `"should not happen"` whenever a type-decl's RHS resolved to a still-unsealed `*DeclaredType`. That state is legitimate during mutual type-decl recursion — e.g. `type T1 struct{Next *T2}; type T2 T1` — where neither half is sealed until both `TRANS_LEAVE` handlers fire.

The removed guard documented an invariant the type system does not actually maintain. `dt.sealed` is a one-bit lifecycle marker for "Base/Methods filled in" (see `checkSeal` doc at `types.go:1961`), not a precondition for slot lookup. Sites that need a sealed type already gate explicitly (`type_check.go:440`, `realm.go:1788`).

## Changes

- `gnovm/pkg/gnolang/preprocess.go` — drop the stale `panic("should not happen")` on unsealed `*DeclaredType` in `tryPredefine`.
- `gnovm/tests/files/decltype_mutual.gno` — filetest exercising the mutual cycle.

## Test plan

- [x] `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/ -run 'TestFiles/decltype_mutual' -test.short`
- [x] Wider filetests pass.","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5763,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): ignore blank fields in struct equality and map keys
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["omarsy"],"body":"## Summary

Fixes Go 1.17 spec mismatches in Gno struct equality and map keys.

The Go spec says struct equality compares corresponding non-blank fields. Gno was also comparing fields named `_`, so values like:

```go
struct{ _ int; X int }{1, 2} == struct{ _ int; X int }{3, 2}
```

incorrectly evaluated to `false`.

Gno also encoded blank struct fields into map keys, so a lookup such as:

```go
m := map[struct{ _ int; X int }]string{{1, 2}: "a"}
m[struct{ _ int; X int }{3, 2}]
```

missed the existing entry even though the keys are equal under the Go spec.

This updates `StructKind` equality and struct map-key encoding to skip blank fields while preserving existing comparability checks. A companion negative test verifies that blank fields still count for comparability, so a struct containing `_ []int` remains non-comparable.

Spec reference:
https://go.googlesource.com/go/+/refs/tags/go1.17.5/doc/go_spec.html#Comparison_operators

## Tests

```bash
go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestFiles/types/cmp_struct_[hi]' -count=1 -v
go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestFiles/types/cmp_struct' -count=1
go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestFiles/(map48|types/cmp_struct_h|types/cmp_struct_i)' -count=1 -v
go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestFiles/map4' -count=1
go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestComputeMapKey' -count=1
git diff --check
```

Note: a full `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -count=1` was started before the map-key follow-up but did not complete after several minutes, so it was stopped.

## AI Disclosure

This PR was prepared with AI assistance.

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): bound print/println output to prevent native memory exhaustion
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["thehowl"],"body":"Builds on #5155 by @davd-gzl, extending it to fully close the reported print/println memory-exhaustion vector and unifying the value-rendering code.

## Problem

`print`/`println` of an attacker-crafted value can exhaust a validator's native (Go) memory, bypassing the GnoVM allocator. The rendering builds `[]string` slices and joins them (and formats via `fmt.Sprintf`) *before* any gas is charged for the output, so the allocation is unbounded and untracked.

Two shapes matter:
- **Flat:** a single large collection (e.g. `make([]int, N)`).
- **Nested (worse):** a structure that is *cheap to build* but expands multiplicatively when printed — shared children re-expanded at every sibling, `O(breadth^depth)`. This is cheap within gas to construct yet renders to gigabytes.

Note `bounded_strings.go` (`boundedBuf`, added later on master) does **not** cover this: by its own design it is the validator **panic-recovery** path only (`BoundedPanicRender`, structural-only, no user `String()`/`Error()`). The trusted `print`/`println`/`Sprint`/`String` path was still unbounded.

## Fix

Route all value→string rendering through a single size-capped `boundedBuilder`:

- Every write is checked against a global `printOutputLimit` (64 KB); once reached it appends `...(truncated)` and renderers stop descending. This bounds total work to `O(printOutputLimit)` **regardless of nesting or breadth** — closing both the flat and the nested-explosion cases on the path `println` actually uses.
- The per-collection `printLimit` (256) cap from #5155 is kept for the readable `slice[...(N elements)]` / `map{...(N entries)}` messages and to avoid hex-formatting huge byte arrays.

Refactor folded into the same change:
- Unified the duplicated `String`/`Sprint`/`ProtectedString`/`ProtectedSprint` families into `writeWrapped` / `writeSprint` that write into the buffer.
- `ArrayValue` and `SliceValue` (a window into an `ArrayValue`) now share `writeArrayContents`.
- `boundedBuilder` implements `io.Writer`, so rendering uses `fmt.Fprintf` directly instead of `writeString(fmt.Sprintf(...))`, dropping an intermediate string allocation per formatted write.

## Behavior change

`print`/`println` output (and panic-message rendering) is now capped at 64 KB with a trailing `...(truncated)` marker. All existing filetest golden outputs are unchanged (same `fmt` verbs; verified across the full `TestFiles` corpus). Also adopts master's removal of the `readonly(...)` print wrapper.

## Relationship to `bounded_strings.go`

Complementary, not redundant: `boundedBuf` stays the structural-only renderer for the untrusted panic-recovery path; this bounds the trusted print/`Sprint`/`String` path, which *does* invoke user `String()`/`Error()` and so cannot reuse it. The design note in `bounded_strings.go` is updated to reflect this.

## Testing

- New unit tests: `boundedBuilder` cap behavior, per-collection truncation (byte/non-byte array, slice, map), and a regression test that the nested shared-reference structure (previously OOM/timeout) now renders bounded and instantly.
- `print4.gno` filetest for slice/array/map truncation.
- Full `gnovm/pkg/gnolang` suite + `TestFiles` corpus pass (only pre-existing, environmental Go-version `TypeCheckError` wording diffs remain).

## Credits

Original fix and tests by @davd-gzl (#5155). Addresses the security report referenced there.

nodes.title: fix(tm2): make test p2p clusters dial each pair in one direction only
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["thehowl"],"body":"## Problem

`ci / tm2` has failed on every `master` push since June 4 (`ecc5c9fe`, `11b05a42`, `4b3479e1`, `ba9da8eb`), always the same way:

```
--- FAIL: TestReactorDoesNotResignKnownSelfPrevote (300.04s)
panic: Timed out waiting for all validators to commit a block
```

The failing commits are uncorrelated with the failure (docs-only, CI config, gnovm), and the identical tree passed `tm2` twice on the #5216 PR branch hours before failing on master — so this is a flake, not a regression. It reproduces locally at roughly 1 in 3 runs.

## Root cause

In `MakeConnectedPeers` (`tm2/pkg/internal/p2p/p2p.go`), every switch dials every other switch concurrently. For each pair there's a racy interleaving:

1. A's outbound dial to B completes → A registers peer B, fires `PeerConnected`.
2. B's outbound dial to A completes → B registers peer A, fires `PeerConnected`.
3. `MakeConnectedPeers` sees both events and returns "all connected".
4. B's accept loop sees A's inbound conn → rejects it as a duplicate (`switch.go` "already connected") → closes the TCP conn backing **A's registered peer** → A removes peer B.
5. Symmetrically, A rejects B's inbound → B removes peer A.

Both directions are torn down *after* setup reported success, and test peers aren't persistent, so nothing redials. Goroutine dumps from hung runs confirm it: zero gossip/MConnection routines, both switches idle in `waitForPeersToDial`.

`TestReactorDoesNotResignKnownSelfPrevote` (added in #5348) is the test that pays for this because it's the only reactor test with a 2-validator net that must commit a block — losing the single link is a total partition. N=4 tests survive losing one link, which is why this raced silently before.

## Fix

Dial only peers with a higher index, so each pair connects in exactly one direction and the duplicate-rejection path can never trigger. Inbound and outbound connections both fire `PeerConnected`, so each switch still observes `Count-1` events before setup returns. Also replaces the concurrent `sws = append(...)` (raced across the errgroup goroutines and made slot order nondeterministic) with an indexed write.

## Verification

- Before: 2/6 local runs of `TestReactorDoesNotResignKnownSelfPrevote` hang until panic.
- After: 0/20 local runs fail.
- All reactor tests in `tm2/pkg/bft/consensus`, plus `tm2/pkg/bft/blockchain` and `tm2/pkg/bft/mempool` (the other `MakeConnectedPeers` users) pass.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :globe_with_meridians:  tendermint v2"],"number":5781,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: docs(gnovm): document pointer equality divergence for zero-sized types
nodes.data_json: {"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"- Documents that Gno's pointer `==` is uniform `(Base, Index)` identity for every element type, whereas gc-Go folds some zero-sized cases (`runtime.zerobase`, offset arithmetic on `\u0026arr[i]` / `\u0026s.f`), so comparisons gc-Go may report `true` are always `false` in Gno.\n- Adds a \"Pointer equality for zero-sized types\" section to `docs/resources/go-gno-compatibility.md` (with a per-line `Gno / gc-Go` comparison) plus a memory-model note, and cross-referencing comments at `PointerValue` (`values.go`), `doOpRef` (`op_expressions.go`), and `new` (`uverse.go`).\n- Each example was verified against Gno master and gc-Go go1.25.1; the gc-Go heap cases are marked `unspecified` since their result is escape-analysis dependent — when the operands don't escape (e.g. the literal `new(T) == new(T)` compared in place) gc-Go keeps them distinct and folds to `false`; only when both escape to the heap do they route to `runtime.zerobase` and compare `true`.","kind":"pr","labels":[":book:  documentation",":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5771,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: perf(ci): speed up the gno.land test job (in-memory parallel txtars + per-package fixes)
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["thehowl"],"body":"## Summary

Speeds up the `ci / gnoland` **`main / test`** job, which had become a CI bottleneck. The headline change runs the `gno.land` txtar integration suite **in-memory and in parallel**; on top of that, the three next-slowest test packages (`gnoweb/markdown`, `gnoweb`, `cmd/gnoland`) are sped up, and a chunk of now-dead subprocess machinery is removed.

Builds on #5748 (which switched the txtar integration tests from a subprocess-node-per-txtar to a single **in-memory** process, still sequential). Job timeline: ~22 min (original) → ~10 min (#5748, sequential) → **~7.2 min (this PR, green on CI)**. `main / test` is now bounded by the single longest test package (`gno.land/pkg/integration`, ~410 s on the runner) rather than several packages; pushing lower would require caching preprocessed packages across in-memory node boots, which is left as future work.

Local `gno.land/pkg/integration` test-exec time (coverage on), for the in-memory parallel switch:

| config | 4 cores |
|---|---|
| original (subprocess, sequential) | 1204 s |
| #5748 (in-memory, sequential) | 468 s |
| **this PR (in-memory, parallel)** | **177 s** |

Parallel in-memory was disabled long ago (#4502 / #3396: "watch them fail") because of shared-state nondeterminism. Two such issues remained; this PR fixes both, then makes in-memory parallel the default and drops the now-redundant `INMEMORY_TS` toggle.

## Fixes that unblock parallel

**1. Deterministic GC gas (`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/garbage_collector.go`).**
The GC dedups visited objects with a per-object `LastGCCycle` field vs a per-machine `GCCycle` counter. The global `.uverse` `PackageValue` (a process-global singleton via `SetCachePackage(Uverse())`) is shared across machines, so under parallel execution one machine's GC stamps its cycle onto it and another machine's GC — with a colliding cycle number — skips it. That made the GC visit count, and thus GC gas, non-deterministic across parallel nodes (e.g. `gc.txtar` flaked between two gas values).

`.uverse` contents are *already* excluded from GC traversal. This extends that to the visit count: the uverse package value and its block are no longer counted, so GC gas never depends on the shared object's per-machine bookkeeping. The exclusion is bounded to exactly those two container objects.

*Gas note:* uverse (a shared, always-reachable singleton) is no longer counted in per-tx GC gas — a tiny, uniform, deterministic reduction. Only `gc.txtar`'s expected value changes (`sdk/vm` Gas tests and the gnovm Files tests are unaffected).

**2. Port-reuse race on restart (`gno.land/pkg/integration/process.go`).**
`node.Start` mutates the listen address from `:0` to the concrete resolved port; the harness reuses the same config across `gnoland restart`, so a restart re-bound that specific port — which a concurrent node could already hold ("address already in use"). Reset RPC/P2P listen addresses to `:0` on every (re)start so the kernel always picks a free port.

## Per-package test speedups

The same "boot/preprocess once instead of per-test" idea applied to the next-slowest packages in the job (their before-times are from the CI per-package breakdown):

- **`gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/markdown` — 426 s → ~1 s** (`sanitize_integration_test.go`). `TestSanitizeIntegration` spun up a fresh `Machine` + transaction store per txtar case (165 of them) and `RunMemPackage`'d the driver each time, re-preprocessing the `sanitize/v0` + stdlib import chain from source on every case (pprof: `transcribe` ~100% of runtime). Now the driver is preprocessed once and the shared `Machine` is reused — safe because the sanitize helpers are pure string→string with only `init`-populated read-only state.
- **`gno.land/pkg/gnoweb` — 302 s → ~24 s** (`app_test.go`). The four app tests each booted a full in-memory node (every boot loads + runs all example packages from genesis). They only issue read-only GETs, so one node is shared across them, booted lazily (mock-based handler/renderer tests skip the boot) and torn down in `TestMain`.
- **`gno.land/cmd/gnoland` — ~76 s → ~30 s** (`start_test.go`). The three `TestStart_Lazy` cases each start a full node and ran sequentially; they now run in parallel. Concurrent starts collided on the default P2P listen address (`:26656`), so `prepareNodeRPC` now binds `p2p.laddr` to a free port too (mirroring what it already did for RPC); the per-node deadline is bumped to absorb CPU contention.

## Other changes

- `testscript_gnoland.go`: remove the now-dead `commandKindBin` machinery (`buildGnoland`, the `buildOnce`/`gnolandBin` build step, `envKeyExecBin`). The txtar suite only runs in-memory, so building a separate gnoland binary per run was unreachable; an unset command kind now defaults to in-memory. `commandKindTesting` / `RunNodeProcess` / `RunNode` (exercised by `TestNodeProcess`) are kept.
- `process_test.go`: widen `TestNodeProcess`'s context deadline 20 s → 60 s. It spawns a subprocess node; under CI CPU contention (other packages' node-start tests run concurrently) a 20 s timeout flaked even though the node starts in a few seconds when idle.
- `testdata_test.go`: in-memory is now the only mode and parallel is the default; deletes the `INMEMORY_TS` env toggle. `SEQ_TS` still forces sequential (for debugging/profiling).
- `_ci-go.yml` / `ci-dir-gnoland.yml`: drop the now-redundant `tests-ts-inmemory` input/flag.
- `node_testing.go`: hoist the `:0` listen address to a package const (`defaultListenAddr`), reused by the port-reset above.
- `tm2/pkg/store/trace/trace_on.go`: guard the gas-tracer writer with a mutex (build-tag–gated debug code; its unsynchronized `bufio.Writer` corrupts under parallel nodes).
- `gno.land/Makefile`: update the test help text.

## Validation

- `ci / gnoland` green; `main / test` ~7.2 min, no OOM on the runner (parallelism is bounded by the runner's ~4 cores, so concurrent nodes are far fewer than on a dev machine).
- `gc.txtar` deterministic across parallel runs; full integration suite passes in parallel.
- `go test ./gno.land/pkg/sdk/vm/ -run Gas`: pass. `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/ -run Files -short`: only the pre-existing `or_f0` golden mismatch (present on master too).

nodes.title: method expressions don't support interface, promoted, or mixed-receiver forms
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":[],"body":"GnoVM only supports method expressions (`T.M`, `(*T).M`) when `M` is a method declared
directly on `T` *and* the receiver form written in the expression matches the declared
receiver. All other forms — all valid Go since Go 1.0 — diverge:

| Method expression | Go | GnoVM |
|---|---|---|
| `T.M`, value receiver | ✅ `func(T)` | ✅ works |
| `(*T).M`, pointer receiver | ✅ `func(*T)` | ✅ works |
| `(*T).M`, **value** receiver | ✅ `func(*T)`, derefs receiver | ❌ resolves to `func(T)`; the valid call `f(&t)` is rejected at preprocess |
| `T.M`, **pointer** receiver | ❌ compile error (`invalid method expression T.M (needs pointer receiver (*T).M)`) | ⚠️ accepted as `func(*T)` and runs¹ |
| promoted: `T.M`, `M` from an embedded field | ✅ | ❌ `unknown *DeclaredType method named M` (#5777) |
| interface: `I.M` | ✅ dynamic dispatch on first arg | ❌ `unknown *DeclaredType method named M` |

¹ caught by the go/types typecheck in `gno lint` / `gno test` / addpkg
(`gnoTypeCheckError`), but accepted by the VM itself (`gno run`).

### Steps to reproduce

Each verified with `gno run` vs `go run`:

```go
// 1. REJECTED but valid Go: (*T).M with value receiver.
//    Gno: "cannot use *main.T as int" at the call — f got type func(T) int.
type T int
func (t T) M() int { return int(t) + 1 }
f := (*T).M // Go: func(*T) int
t := T(41)
println(f(&t)) // Go: 42 — Gno: preprocess panic
```

```go
// 2. ACCEPTED but invalid Go: T.M with pointer receiver.
type T int
func (t *T) M() int { return int(*t) + 1 }
f := T.M // Go: compile error — Gno: func(*T) int
t := T(41)
println(f(&t)) // Gno: 42
```

```go
// 3. REJECTED but valid Go: interface method expression.
type I interface{ M() int }
type T int
func (T) M() int { return 42 }
f := I.M // Go: func(I) int — Gno: "unknown *DeclaredType method named M"
println(f(T(0)))
```

```go
// 4. REJECTED but valid Go: promoted method expression (see #5777).
type U int
func (U) M() int { return 42 }
type T struct{ U }
println(T.M(T{})) // Go: 42 — Gno: "unknown *DeclaredType method named M"
```

### Root cause

All four divergences share one root: the `*TypeType` selector branch in preprocess1
([`preprocess.go:2610`](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/4c9de5225/gnovm/pkg/gnolang/preprocess.go#L2610))
resolves the selector via `dt.GetUnboundPathForName(n.Sel)`
([`types.go:2146`](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/4c9de5225/gnovm/pkg/gnolang/types.go#L2146)),
and the expression's static type becomes the raw method `FuncType` — first parameter =
*declared* receiver, regardless of the type written in the expression. Hence:

* The written receiver form is never checked (rows 3–4 of the table): `T.M` /
  `(*T).M` both yield the declared signature.
* `GetUnboundPathForName` only consults `dt.lookupMethod`, i.e. `dt.Methods` — direct
  concrete methods. Interface method sets live on the underlying `*InterfaceType`, and
  promoted methods require a `FindEmbeddedFieldType`-style fallback, so both panic.

Per the spec ([Method expressions](https://go.dev/ref/spec#Method_expressions)), the
derived function's first parameter must be exactly the type written in the expression;
`(*T).M` on a value-receiver method must synthesize a dereferencing wrapper, and
interface method expressions dispatch dynamically. The runtime `VPField` unbound-method
dispatch indexes directly into `dt.Methods` (values.go), so the interface and promoted
cases likely need a dedicated value path or synthesized wrappers, not just a lookup fix.

### Related

* #5777 — the promoted-method case, filed from the gocorpus `ddd.go` run.
* Discovered while reviewing #5707 (method expression deps in init order analysis); all
  forms above fail or resolve before reaching the code added there.

nodes.title: gnovm: a `/p/` package can't take a `realm` as its only/first parameter
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":[],"body":"A function or method whose first parameter is a `realm` is treated as a "crossing function", and crossing functions are rejected outside `/r/` realms. So a `/p/` package can't declare a helper that simply takes the current realm:

```go
package realmbug // gno.land/p/demo/realmbug — a /p/ package

func Sync(rlm realm) {}            // ❌ first realm argument must have name `cur`
func Sync(cur realm) {}            // ❌ crossing function declared in non-realm package

type S struct{}
func (s *S) Sync(rlm realm) {}     // ❌ same — the receiver doesn't make realm "non-first"
```

The only way to compile is to push the realm out of first position with a throwaway param:

```go
func Sync(_ struct{}, rlm realm) {} // ✅ compiles, but that junk arg can't be right
```

This was normal, intended behavior before. Under the old design packages never crossed, so a realm-first signature in `/p/` was always a mistake, and rejecting it was correct. The new interrealm design is what shifts it: now that packages legitimately cross, the same rule blocks a valid pattern and forces the junk-param workaround.

daokit's interrealm-v2 port ([samouraiworld/gnodaokit#64](https://github.com/samouraiworld/gnodaokit/pull/64)) is a real example: it threads a realm through `/p/` package code but has to keep it out of first position everywhere, e.g. `Execute(id uint64, rlm realm)` and `New(conf *Config, rlm realm)`, naming it `rlm` per the reserved-name rule. Where the realm is the only thing a call needs, it's a genuine helper that can't be written as `func(rlm realm)`.

## Where it comes from

`FuncType.IsCrossing()` ([types.go:1382](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/master/gnovm/pkg/gnolang/types.go#L1382)) keys purely off the first parameter's type and ignores the receiver, and `crossingAllowed()` ([preprocess.go:4499](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/master/gnovm/pkg/gnolang/preprocess.go#L4499)) rejects crossing functions outside `/r/` realms (with carve-outs for `*_test.gno` and ephemeral `main`).

## Repro

(needs Go, ~1 min)

```bash
mkdir realmbug && cd realmbug
printf 'module = "gno.land/p/demo/realmbug"\ngno = "0.9"\n' > gnomod.toml
printf 'package realmbug\n\nfunc Sync(rlm realm) {}\n' > bug.gno
go run github.com/gnolang/gno/gnovm/cmd/gno@master lint .
```

## Question

Now that packages can cross, should a `/p/` package be allowed a `realm` as its sole/first arg (named e.g. `rlm`, non-crossing), or is the junk-leading-param still the intended workaround?
","kind":"issue","labels":["🐞 bug",":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5786,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): respect type identity in assignability
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["omarsy"],"body":"## Summary

- add exact Go-style type identity checks for assignment, comparison, function signatures, interface method matching, and debug assertions
- keep explicit conversion on the Go rule that ignores struct tags, while still respecting embedded fields, map values, and variadic function signatures
- add filetests for struct tags, embedded fields, map element types, variadic functions, interface methods, and struct comparison
- add `gnovm/adr/prxxxx_type_identity.md` documenting the identity split

## Root Cause

Gno reused `TypeID()` for both conversion compatibility and exact identity checks. `TypeID()` intentionally ignores struct tags, which is correct for conversions, but assignment and comparison need exact type identity. That also hid related identity gaps around embedded fields, map element types, and variadic function signatures.

nodes.title: feat(gnoweb): add omnibar search for realms, packages and users
nodes.data_json: {"assignees":["alexiscolin"],"body":"Turns the gnoweb search bar into an omnibar: as you type, a dropdown surfaces matching Realm (/r/), Pure (/p/) and Users (/u/), plus an in-page content search of the current realm/source view. Discovery is powered entirely by the chain (vm/qpaths), no indexer; gnoweb stays stateless and full on-chain. ## What's new - Type-as-you-go discovery grouped into Realms / Pures / Users, ranked by relevance (a match in the realm name outranks one in the namespace/address), capped to the top 5 per group. - Type tags (r / p / u) so each result's kind is obvious at a glance. - In-page search ("This page" section): finds and highlights matches in the current realm's rendered content / source. - Keyboard navigation (↑/↓/⏎/Esc) with an ARIA combobox/listbox. - Select-on-focus: the prefilled path is selected on focus so typing replaces it (mirrors the breadcrumb behaviour). - Seamless UI: the dropdown reads as the bar extending downward (shared surface, squared join via :has()), dark-mode aware, design-tokens only. - Graceful degradation: without JS the bar still works as a direct path navigator (Enter submits). ## Architecture gnoweb stays a thin, stateless proxy: - GET /search.json returns {realms, packages} fetched live via vm/qpaths (existing ClientAdapter.ListPaths). A single cacheable URL, identical for all clients (Cache-Control: public, max-age=30). - The browser fetches the list once (lazily) and filters / ranks / renders client-side, no query hits the node per keystroke. - A new RealmDirectory interface is the seam behind which the source can evolve (live RPC today → node-side search later) without touching the handler or the UI. Scaling (follow-up, on-brand) Discovery is complete up to the node's qpaths default (1000 paths). Beyond that, the plan is a node-side search (vm/qsearch / a local off-consensus index, à la tx_index) that gnoweb queries: keeping gnoweb stateless and the index on-chain. It's a backend swap behind RealmDirectory, not a rewrite. ## Changes ### Feature: backend - gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/realm_directory.go (new): RealmDirectory interface + RPC-backed impl (semaphore-bounded, parallel /r + /p). - gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/handler_search.go (new): /search.json handler. - gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/app.go: route registration. ### Feature: frontend - frontend/js/controller-searchbar.ts: omnibar controller (fetch-once, filter/rank, grouped render, type tags, keyboard nav, in-page search, select-on-focus). - components/layouts/header.html: dropdown container + ARIA combobox wiring. - frontend/css/06-blocks.css: .b-omnisearch styles (seamless dropdown, type tags, sections); tokens only. - frontend/postcss.config.cjs: PurgeCSS dynamicAttributes so [data-type] tag styles survive minification. - public/js/controller-searchbar.js, public/main.css: rebuilt assets. ### Known limitations / follow-ups - Capped at the node's qpaths default (1000); rpcClient.ListPaths doesn't forward limit (pre-existing). Forwarding it (→10000) + node-side search are the scaling follow-ups. - Fuzzy = substring match on the path (client-side), beyond a simple relevance score. Screenshots _Part of #5464 (package discovery) and #5461 (discoverability on gno.land)._ ","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :mountain: gno.land","🌍 gnoweb"],"number":5761,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: gnovm: map[interface{}]V with uncomparable dynamic key — wrong message + uncatchable host panic
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":[],"body":"## Brief description

`map[interface{}]V` operations (`m[k]`, `m[k] = v`, `delete(m, k)`) where the *dynamic* key type is uncomparable diverge from Go in two ways:

1. **Wrong/inconsistent panic message** — Gno reports either the static key constraint (`slice type cannot be used as map key`) or the inner-leaf type, not the dynamic type carried by the interface. Go always reports `runtime error: hash of unhashable type <outer dynamic type>`.
2. **Uncatchable host panic for several shapes** — Gno's `ComputeMapKey` falls through to a raw `panic(fmt.Sprintf(...))` for map / func / nested cases (values.go default branch). This is a Go-level panic, not a Gno `&Exception{}`, so user `recover()` cannot catch it and the VM exits.

(2) is the serious one: a Gno realm using `map[interface{}]V` cannot reliably guard against bad keys via `recover()`.

## Reproduction

```go
// run
package main

func main() {
	defer func() {
		r := recover()
		println("recover:", r)
	}()
	m := map[interface{}]int{}
	m[map[int]int{}] = 1
}
```

**Go output:**
```
recover: runtime error: hash of unhashable type map[int]int
```

**Gno output:**
```
panic running expression main(): unexpected map key type map[int]int
exit status 1
```

The Gno `recover()` never runs — the VM dies.

## Full divergence catalogue

| dynamic key type | Go message | Gno message | Recoverable in Gno? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `[]int` | `hash of unhashable type []int` | `slice type cannot be used as map key` | ✓ |
| `struct{[]int}` (= `main.S`) | `hash of unhashable type main.S` | `slice type cannot be used as map key` | ✓ |
| `map[int]int` | `hash of unhashable type map[int]int` | `unexpected map key type map[int]int` | ✗ host panic |
| `func()` | `hash of unhashable type func()` | `unexpected map key type func()` | ✗ host panic |
| `[1]map[int]int` | `hash of unhashable type [1]map[int]int` | `unexpected map key type map[int]int` | ✗ host panic |

Comparable dynamic types (`int`, comparable struct, etc.) work correctly: dedup, type-tagged distinction, lookup — all match Go.

## Root cause pointer

`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/values.go` — `(*TypedValue).ComputeMapKey`:

- `case *SliceType:` panics with the wrong message (`"slice type cannot be used as map key"`).
- `default:` (covers `*MapType`, `*FuncType`, `*InterfaceType` not statically pinned) does `panic(fmt.Sprintf("unexpected map key type %s", tv.T.String()))` — host-level, uncatchable, and reports leaf not outer.

Interface-typed keys don't seem to have a path that unwraps the dynamic type and recurses with the correct type-name carried for the eventual panic.

## Suggested fix

1. When the static key constraint is `interface`, unwrap and recurse using the dynamic type — and remember that dynamic type for the eventual panic message (parallel to the `dynType` capture done in `isEql` in #PR — see [op_binary.go isEql](../blob/master/gnovm/pkg/gnolang/op_binary.go)).
2. Replace the `*SliceType` / `default` raw panics with `panic(&Exception{Value: typedString("runtime error: hash of unhashable type " + dynType.String())})` so they are Gno-catchable and message-matched to Go.
3. Add filetests under `gnovm/tests/files/types/` covering the table above (slice / struct{slice} / map / func / [1]map dynamic types).

## Related

Companion to the in-flight `==` / `!=` runtime cmp check work (which fixed the analogous "comparing uncomparable type" path). The map-key path is the next bucket with the same theme.","kind":"issue","labels":[],"number":5783,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: [codex] fix(gnovm): preserve readonly on alias-retaining copies
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["omarsy"],"body":"## Summary

This is an alternative follow-up to #5747.

#5747 fixed #5736 by making array/struct copies use type-driven PkgID stamping, so value-only `/p/` types such as `uint256.Uint` can be copied and mutated locally. That part is preserved here.

The unsafe part is that #5747 also removed readonly provenance completely. For alias-retaining value copies, the copied wrapper can be fresh while a child reference still points into foreign storage:

```go
// /r/victim
var Global = [1][]byte{[]byte("safe")}
func Copy(dst []byte, src string) { copy(dst, []byte(src)) }

// /r/attacker
x := victim.Global
victim.Copy(x[0], "pwn!")
```

Calling back into a victim-declared helper borrows `m.Realm` to the victim realm, so a pure ownership check permits the write unless the retained alias still carries readonly provenance.

## Change

- Restore `N_Readonly` provenance on foreign reads through selector, index, slice, deref, and address-of.
- Keep #5747's type-driven PkgID stamping for `ArrayValue.Copy` and `StructValue.Copy`.
- Clear readonly provenance only for transitively alias-free copies: primitives, arrays of alias-free values, and structs whose fields are all alias-free.
- Preserve readonly provenance for copies that may retain references, including `[1][]byte`.
- Add a txtar repro for the alias round-trip and update interrealm v2 docs/fixtures.
- Add an ADR at `gnovm/adr/prxxxx_readonly_alias_roundtrip.md`.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `go test ./gno.land/pkg/integration -run 'TestTestdata/(issue_5747_alias_roundtrip|interrealm_v2)' -count=1 -v`
- `(cd examples/gno.land/r/tests/issue5736_bar && go run ../../../../../gnovm/cmd/gno test .)`
- `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -run 'TestFiles' -count=1 -short`
- `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang -count=1 -short`

nodes.title: feat(gnovm)!: remove N_Readonly provenance taint, fix cross-realm /p/-type arithmetic
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["moul"],"body":"Fixes #5736.

## TL;DR

A `/r/` realm calling a helper that does in-place `uint256`-style arithmetic on a
value handed in from another `/r/` realm panicked with `cannot directly modify
readonly tainted object`, even when the value was freshly allocated inside the
executing realm. Two mechanisms caused this; this PR removes the whole class of
provenance protection behind them and keeps the one layer that actually closes
laundering — the **PkgID ownership gate at the pointer-deref boundary**
(`PopAsPointer2` → `IsReadonly`).

The PR has two parts:

1. **Type-driven Copy stamping** (the original #5736 fix): `{Array,Struct}Value.Copy`
   no longer propagates the *source's* runtime PkgID into the destination. Authority
   is now type-driven (mirroring `stampPkgID`'s split rule from #5706): a `/r/`-declared
   type still inherits its `/r/` owner; a `/p/`-declared type keeps the fresh
   `currentRealmID` stamp, so an in-place value-copy belongs to the realm doing the copy.

2. **Removal of the N_Readonly provenance taint** (breaking): the sticky read-time taint
   layer is deleted entirely. Direct cross-realm writes remain blocked by the ownership
   gate; the provenance-only protections (value-copy laundering, borrow+alias laundering,
   type-pun conversions) are intentionally dropped and become the realm author's
   responsibility.

## Why the taint doesn't hold up (Morgan's `copy()` argument)

Credit to @morgan for the framing that reshaped this PR.

> so Array/StructValue.Copy copies over the PkgID? It doesn't look right.
>
> Also because for arrays, you could just do `copy(dst, src)`, and I imagine that
> doesn't copy over the PkgID.
>
> Structs, you'd have to copy things manually, the only concern is if with a copy you
> can create a hijack by "writing" to unexported fields you're not supposed to. That
> said, they can only be modified in bulk, so I don't know if that's really a concern.

The Copy-time hardening fired on value-assignment syntax (`*z = *x`) but **not** on the
equivalent `copy(dst, src)` builtin — same semantic operation, different stamping based
on syntactic form. It was a speed-bump, not a defense. And the unexported-field-hijack
concern (`*foreignPtr = T{}`) is blocked at the pointer-deref write check, not at Copy.
So the real defense lives at the pointer-deref boundary, which is untouched; removing the
provenance layer opens no new attack vector.

## The bug

```
panic: cannot directly modify readonly tainted object
  gno.land/p/onbloc/uint256/bitwise.gno:89  (z.arr[0] = z.arr[0] << n)
  gno.land/r/.../common/common.gno:11        (u256.Zero().Lsh(x, 1))
  gno.land/r/.../bar/bar.gno:9               (common.DoLsh(NewUint(123), 1))
```

Repro fixture added under `examples/gno.land/r/tests/issue5736_{common,bar}`.

## What changed (29 files)

**VM core (`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/`)**
- `values.go` — type-driven PkgID stamping in `{Array,Struct}Value.Copy`; delete the
  `N_Readonly` sentinel + `IsReadonly`/`SetReadonly`/`WithReadonly`; drop `cp.N = tv.N`.
- `op_expressions.go` — drop read-time taint propagation in `doOpIndex1/2`, `doOpSelector`,
  `doOpSlice`, `doOpStar`, `doOpRef`; keep the `doOpConvert` Case-1 guard (now a pure
  ownership check).
- `ownership.go` — drop the `tv.IsReadonly()` short-circuit in `IsReadonlyBy`; the PkgID
  ownership gate is retained.
- `machine.go` — drop N_Readonly handling from `IsReadonly`; comments refreshed.
- `values_string.go` — drop `readonly()` wrapping in `ProtectedString`.

**Docs**
- `docs/resources/gno-interrealm-v2.md` — remove the "§8 Readonly Taint" section and update

**Filetest golden refreshes (`gnovm/tests/files/`)**
- `zrealm_slice0/slice1/map3` — append/copy/map writes to current-realm-owned storage now

- `zrealm_crossrealm4/21/22` — the `ReaDoNLY` sentinel is no longer serialized into realm dumps.
- `zrealm_launder_*` (a_value_copy, pdata_ptr_deref, pdata_value_copy, rdata_conv_iface_box,

**Example fixtures (`examples/`)**

- `r/gnoland/boards2/v1/hub/filetests/z_0_b`, `z_4_a` — golden updates from the taint removal.

## Verification

- `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/ -run Files` (+`-test.short`) — passes
- `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/` (full unit suite) — passes
- `go test ./gno.land/pkg/integration/ -run 'TestTestdata/(interrealm_v2|addpkg_private)'` — passes
- New fixture `examples/gno.land/r/tests/issue5736_bar` — passes with this patch, panics without
- Reconciled with master's #5758 ("close the nil-realm cross-realm write hole"): #5758's
  frozen-realm fix and the stdlib own-write `ownPkgID` exemption are preserved; only the taint
  pieces are removed. #5758's nil-realm/launder filetests pass.

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): typedRuntimeError for runtime errors
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["Villaquiranm"],"body":"fixes: #5667 
This PR:
1. Introduces a new uverse type (.runtimeError) — is supposed to be a structure type that implements error interface
2. Changes recover() semantics — previously recover() at these sites returns a string; now it returns a .runtimeError struct. That's a
  user-visible breaking change in Gno panic/recover behavior.
3. Only migrates one of ~20 panic sites — op_binary.go, values.go have many more typedString("runtime error: ...") calls that still emit strings.
   This creates an inconsistency: some runtime panics are error-assertable, others aren't.
   
On the issue we have a divergence between Go and Gno behaviour.
I Initially thought the problem was that the recover was not recovering the panic, but actually there is a differencei in the way go issues runtime panics. It uses a type implementing the error interface instead of a plain string as we do.

This PR is incomplete as before merging we should migrate all occurences of `typedString("runtime error:")...`","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm","review/triage-pending"],"number":5732,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

revert(validators): remove valset trust-level and cooldown limits
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["omarsy"],"body":"## Summary

Revert the chain-side validator-set trust-level and cooldown limits introduced by #4834.

- remove the trust-level overlap check from `r/sys/validators/v3` proposal execution
- remove the 24-hour cooldown checks and their governance setters
- remove the dedicated limit tests and cooldown-disable setup from integration scenarios
- preserve later validator safeguards such as operator deduplication, KeepRunning enforcement, signing-key re-resolution, and empty-set rejection
- add a regression test proving that a full validator-set replacement is allowed

## Why

The previous implementation applied a Tendermint light-client trust-level rule at the chain governance layer. That rule belongs to light-client verification for non-adjacent headers. For adjacent headers, CometBFT verifies the committed next-validator hash without applying the overlap threshold. If a skipped update cannot be verified directly, a relayer can submit intermediate headers until verification reaches adjacent heights.

The 24-hour cooldown is also not required by the IBC or CometBFT specifications. In particular, it would prevent future AtomOne Interchain Security (ICS) provider-validator-set mirroring from applying updates to gno.land when AtomOne changes its validator set without a matching cooldown constraint. This is separate from IBC light-client verification.

The rationale is documented in `gno.land/adr/pr5767_revert_valset_trust_level_cooldown.md`.

## Validation

- `go run ./gnovm/cmd/gno fmt -w examples/gno.land/r/sys/validators/v3/proposal.gno examples/gno.land/r/sys/validators/v3/proposal_test.gno`
- `go run ./gnovm/cmd/gno test -C examples/gno.land/r/sys/validators/v3 . -v`
- `go test ./gno.land/pkg/integration -run 'TestTestdata/(params_valset_proposal_e2e|params_valset_proposal_power_update|params_valset_proposal_remove|params_valset_keeprunning_optout_bypass)$' -count=1 -v`
- `bash -n misc/val-scenarios/scenarios/18_govdao_v3_add_remove_validator.sh`
- `git show --check --stat --oneline HEAD`

## AI-assisted contribution

This PR was prepared with an AI coding agent. The responsible human owner is @omarsy.

fix(val-scenarios): retry single-node compose up on port bind race
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["D4ryl00"],"body":"## Problem

The `misc/val-scenarios` suite fails intermittently in CI when restarting an individual node. Example from a recent run of `scenario-10-four-validators-safe-reset-two-below-consensus` ([failed job](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/actions/runs/26945868033/job/79499753044)):

```
[scenario-10] chain halted as expected on val1
 Container scenario-10-val3-1  Creating
Error response from daemon: failed to set up container networking: driver failed
programming external connectivity on endpoint scenario-10-val3-1 (...): failed to
bind host port for 0.0.0.0:32770:172.18.0.2:26657/tcp: address already in use
##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.
```

The scenario got through the interesting part (`chain halted as expected`) and died purely on a Docker host-port allocation race — unrelated to anything under test.

## Root cause

The published RPC/control ports are mapped as ephemeral (`"::26657"` / `"::8080"`), so Docker's daemon picks the host port. Its ephemeral allocator can hand a node a port that:

- a **sibling stopped-but-not-removed** container still reserves in the daemon's bookkeeping (`safe_reset` does `compose stop` without `rm`), or
- is briefly held during teardown.

`start_all_nodes` already retried the **group** start on `address already in use`, but the **single-service** paths did not — `start_node` (used by `start_validator`/`start_sentry`) and both sentry IP-rotation restarts. With `set -euo pipefail`, the first `compose up` failure killed the whole scenario.

## Fix

Factor the retry into a `compose_up_one` helper — `compose rm -fs` + `compose up -d`, up to 3 attempts, forcing Docker to pick a fresh port each time — and use it for:

- `start_node` (covers `start_validator` / `start_sentry` restarts)
- signer startup in `start_all_nodes`
- both sentry IP-rotation restarts

Only `address already in use` is retried; any other failure aborts immediately (with the captured output) so genuine startup errors still surface. The existing group-start retries at `start_all_nodes` are left as-is.

This is a CI-infra-only change under `misc/val-scenarios/`; syntax-checked with `bash -n`.","kind":"pr","labels":[],"number":5775,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): allow type-switch with sole `case nil:` (preprocess single-case branch must tag-type-only when ct is nil)
nodes.data_json: {"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"- Before: a type-switch whose sole case was `case nil:` (e.g. `switch xx := x.(type) { case nil: ... }`) panicked at preprocess with `name xx not declared` because the single-case shortcut registered the switch var with `anyValue(ct)` where `ct == nil`; after: the sole-`nil` form is accepted and `xx` is bound to the tag's static type, matching Go.\n- `preprocess1`'s `*TypeSwitchStmt` branch in `gnovm/pkg/gnolang/preprocess.go` now gates the `len(n.Cases) == 1` define-with-case-type shortcut on `ct != nil`, so a nil `ct` falls through to the same tag-type define used for multi-case switches.\n- New filetest `gnovm/tests/files/typeswitch1.gno` mirrors gocorpus's typeswitch1 (sole `case nil:` alongside mixed multi-type cases) and exercises both the `xx := x.(type)` and bare `xx.(type)` shapes.","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5766,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: fix(gnovm): bounds-check array index so recover() catches out-of-range panic
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"## Summary

Indexing an array (or pointer-to-array) past its length raised a native Go `runtime error: index out of range`, which escaped as a host panic and could not be caught by a deferred `recover()` — unlike slice/string OOB (see `recover14`).

`ArrayValue.GetPointerAtIndexInt2` now bounds-checks `ii` on both the `Data == nil` and `Data != nil` branches and panics with a Gno `&Exception{...}`. Placed at the value layer because it's the convergence point for every array-access path (direct index, slice-over-array, pointer rebind, convenience helper) — matching where the slice/string/map checks already live.

## Test plan

- [x] New filetest `gnovm/tests/files/recover25.gno`
- [x] `go test ./gnovm/pkg/gnolang/ -run Files -test.short`","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5738,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: WIP fix(gnovm): clamp bogus make(map, n) hint
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["ltzmaxwell"],"body":"- Before: `make(map[K]V, n)` panicked with the internal `"addition overflow"` when `n` was large enough that `allocMap + allocMapItem*n` overflowed int64; after: such hints (and negatives) silently clamp to 0, matching Go's `makemap` verbatim.
- `Allocator.NewMap` (`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/alloc.go`) clamps `size < 0 || int64(size) > maxMapHint` to 0 before `AllocateMap`. `maxMapHint = (math.MaxInt64 - allocMap) / allocMapItem` is the largest hint whose preallocation cost still fits in int64 (~1.15e17).
- Realistic over-budget hints (e.g. `make(map, 10_000_000_000)`) are well below `maxMapHint`, so the clamp doesn't fire — the full byte cost is charged and `Allocate` panics with `"allocation limit exceeded"` at the `make()` line, keeping the diagnostic clean.
- Covered by filetest `gnovm/tests/files/make19.gno` (negative + MaxInt hints don't panic; map stays usable) and `TestNewMapHintBoundary` in `alloc_test.go` (full byte charge at `pivot-1`/`pivot`; clamp to `allocMap` at `pivot+1`/`MaxInt`/`-1`).","kind":"pr","labels":[":package: :robot: gnovm"],"number":5770,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

nodes.title: feat(gnoweb): serve embedded repository docs under /docs
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["moul","alexiscolin"],"body":"## Summary

Adds a `/docs` route to gnoweb that renders the contents of the repo's `docs/` folder using the existing realm goldmark pipeline. Goal: a documentation surface that is always in sync with the running gnoweb binary (no copy step, no separate deploy), making `docs.gno.land` optional over time.

Draft status, but feature-complete for parity: routing, internal link rewriting, cross-repo GitHub fallback, `:::` admonition syntax, section navigation parsed from `README.md` with current-page highlighting, and content invariants tests.

## Why

- Today, `docs/` is rendered by `docs.gno.land` (Docusaurus, separate repo), deployed asynchronously on push to master. Drift between the docs and a deployed gnoweb is possible, and offline / on-prem users have no built-in doc surface.
- Embedding the `docs/` folder in the gnoweb binary makes documentation a property of the build: `go build ./gno.land/cmd/gnoweb` ships docs that exactly match the code.

## How

Five pieces, each independently testable.

### 1. `docs/` becomes a Go package (`docs/docs.go`)

```go
//go:embed README.md CONSTITUTION.md LAWS.md MANIFESTO.md
//go:embed users/*.md builders/*.md resources/*.md
//go:embed images _assets
var content embed.FS
func FS() fs.FS { return content }
```

`//go:embed` cannot reach files above the importing package, so the embed lives inside `docs/`. One small `.go` file there is the smallest possible footprint. The whitepaper (`.tex`/`.pdf`/`.aux`/`.toc`) and Makefile are deliberately excluded.

### 2. Sidebar parsed from README.md (`docs/sidebar.go`)

`docs.Sidebar()` walks `README.md` once at startup, captures `## …` headings as sections and `- [Title](href) - Summary` bullets as items, and exposes a cached `[]SidebarSection`. External entries (https URLs) are flagged so the renderer can decorate them. README stays the single source of truth for navigation; no sidebar.json to maintain.

### 3. gnoweb handler (`gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/docs.go`)

`DocsHandler` mounted at `/docs` and `/docs/`:

- `/docs` renders `README.md`
- `/docs/builders/getting-started` renders `builders/getting-started.md`
- `/docs/images/...` and `/docs/_assets/...` are served raw via `http.FileServer`
- Resolves both `<path>.md` and `<path>/README.md`
- Reuses `Renderer.RenderRealm` so layout, chroma highlighting, and existing markdown extensions match the rest of gnoweb

### 4. Markdown pre-processing

Two pure text transforms run before `RenderRealm`:

- **`rewriteDocsLinks`**: relative `.md` link `[x](builders/foo.md#section)` becomes `/docs/builders/foo#section`. Images keep their extension. Absolute URLs, anchors-only, and `mailto:` are preserved. Links that escape the `docs/` tree (`../../examples/...`, `../../gnovm/adr/...`) are rewritten to `https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/master/...` so cross-repo references stay clickable.
- **`transformAdmonitions`**: Docusaurus `:::tip Title ... :::` is rewritten line-by-line into the GitHub-style `> [!TIP] Title ... ` blockquote already handled by `markdown/ext_alert.go`. Code-fence aware so `:::` inside code samples is preserved verbatim.

### 5. DocsView component (`components/view_docs.go` + `views/docs.html`)

A new view that places the section navigation in the existing aside slot. The right-side in-page TOC is intentionally dropped for docs pages: consistent cross-page navigation matters more than a per-page outline on short prose content. The active section item gets `is-active` for the highlighted state. CSS additions in `frontend/css/06-blocks.css` (and regenerated `public/main.css`) style the sidebar.

### 6. Route registration (`gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/app.go`)

- Removes the `/docs` → `/u/docs` realm alias (on-chain `/r/docs/*` realms remain accessible at their own paths).
- Mounts `DocsHandler` on `/docs` and `/docs/`.

## Tests

- `docs/docs_test.go` walks every embedded `.md` and verifies that all relative `.md` link targets resolve to an embedded file. 198 internal links checked at PR time, no more silent breakage. Cross-repo escapes are skipped.
- `docs/sidebar_test.go` covers the parser and asserts the real README yields the three canonical categories (`Use Gno.land`, `Build on Gno.land`, `Resources`) and that every internal item points at an embedded `.md`.
- `gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/docs_test.go` table-tests `rewriteDocsLinks` (anchors, parent-relative, GitHub fallback) and `transformAdmonitions` (titles, blank lines inside body, code-fence isolation).
- An httptest pass through the full router asserts:
  - `/docs` renders the README
  - `/docs/` index works (trailing slash)
  - Sub-pages render
  - Internal links emerge as `/docs/...` URLs in the HTML
  - Admonitions render as `gno-alert-*` elements
  - Sidebar section heading visible on `/docs`
  - Sidebar item URLs are clean (no `.md`)
  - Current page item gets the `is-active` class
  - Unknown paths return 404

## docs.gno.land

This PR does NOT touch `deploy-docs.yml` or `misc/docs/`. Both surfaces can run in parallel until parity is confirmed. Once `/docs` is exercised in production, retirement of `docs.gno.land` becomes a separate small decision.

## How to try it

```sh
cd gno.land
make gnoweb.start
# then open http://localhost:8888/docs
```

Run the tests:

```sh
go test ./docs/ ./gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/ -run Docs
go test ./docs/ -run Sidebar
```

## File map

| Category | Files |
|---|---|
| Content embed package | `docs/docs.go` |
| Content invariants | `docs/docs_test.go` |
| Sidebar parser | `docs/sidebar.go`, `docs/sidebar_test.go` |
| Handler, link rewriter, admonition transform | `gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/docs.go` |
| Handler tests | `gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/docs_test.go` |
| Sidebar view | `gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/components/view_docs.go`, `views/docs.html` |
| Sidebar styling | `frontend/css/06-blocks.css`, `public/main.css` |
| Route registration | `gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/app.go` |
","kind":"pr","labels":[":book:  documentation",":package: :mountain:  gno.land","🌍 gnoweb","🚀 ci"],"number":5760,"repo":"gnolang/gno","source":"github"}

feat(gnoweb,boards2): gno-foreign markdown sandbox + rich, paginated comments
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":["jaekwon"],"body":"  What this does

  Adds a safe way to show untrusted markdown — text written by
  other people, like comments — inside a page that a realm
  renders. Then it uses that to give boards2 rich, formatted
  comments that stay safe and load fast even in huge threads.

  The problem

  Realm pages are markdown. When a realm drops user-written
  text straight into that markdown, the text can "break out"
  and inject its own links, images, or HTML. In the normal
  (safe) renderer that means fake or misleading content; with
  HTML rendering turned on it can be a real XSS.

  Until now a realm had two bad options: escape everything (and
  lose all formatting), or trust the input (and risk
  injection). There was no shared, safe way to render someone
  else's markdown inside your own page.

  The main change: a <gno-foreign> sandbox

  A realm wraps untrusted text in a <gno-foreign> block. gnoweb
  then:

  - renders that text in its own isolated, safe pass — no raw
  HTML, links cleaned;
  - marks every link inside as user content (rel="ugc
  nofollow") and removes the first‑party "this is a transaction
  / internal link" icons, so a comment can't disguise itself
  as the realm;
  - still lets normal things work inside, including @user
  mentions (which keep their usual look);
  - limits how many of these blocks one page can render, and
  fails safe (shows a placeholder) past the limit or on a
  malformed block.

  Three pieces support it:

  - gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/markdown — the new sandbox block,
  untrusted‑link handling, and a nesting limit.
  - p/nt/markdown/foreign — a small helper realms call to build
  a correct wrapper (so nobody hand‑builds the envelope
  wrong).
  - chain/markdown stdlib — exposes the per‑page block limit as
  one number, so realms and gnoweb always agree.

  boards2: rich, paginated comments

  boards2 now renders comment bodies through the sandbox, so
  comments can use markdown safely. Big threads are now bounded
  so they stay fast and every comment stays reachable:

  - top‑level comments are paginated (10 per page);
  - any comment shows at most 10 replies inline, with a "View
  all N replies" link that opens a paginated view of just that
  comment's replies;
  - a flat "all comments" view (?flat=1) lists every comment,
  paginated, so even the deepest reply is reachable;
  - the chosen sort order (oldest / newest) is carried through
  all of these views.

  Security fixes (found while reviewing the above)

  - closed several <gno-foreign> escape tricks (e.g.
  </gno-foreign/> and case‑folded tags);
  - cleaned links inside the sandbox (stored XSS);
  - escaped the user‑supplied flag reason and the invalid‑ID
  error message (reflected injection);
  - a frozen thread no longer shows Reply / Edit / Delete links
  the backend would reject;
  - deleting a reply no longer leaves a "ghost" entry in the
  thread.

  Tests

  - 12 new boards2 filetests (pagination, reply limits, flat
  view, and each security fix);
  - 18 new gnoweb markdown golden / unit tests (sandbox,
  untrusted links, mentions).

  Run with:

  - 12 new boards2 filetests (pagination, reply limits, flat view, and each security fix);
  - 18 new gnoweb markdown golden / unit tests (sandbox, untrusted links, mentions).

  Run with:

  go test ./gno.land/pkg/gnoweb/markdown/
  boards2 now renders comment bodies through the sandbox, so comments can use markdown safely. Big threads are
  now bounded so they stay fast and every comment stays reachable:

  - top‑level comments are paginated (10 per page);
  - any comment shows at most 10 replies inline, with a "View all N replies" link that opens a paginated view of
  just that comment's replies;
  - a flat "all comments" view (?flat=1) lists every comment, paginated, so even the deepest reply is reachable;
  - the chosen sort order (oldest / newest) is carried through all of these views.

  Security fixes (found while reviewing the above)

  - closed several <gno-foreign> escape tricks (e.g. </gno-foreign/> and case‑folded tags);
  - cleaned links inside the sandbox (stored XSS);
  - escaped the user‑supplied flag reason and the invalid‑ID error message (reflected injection);
  - a frozen thread no longer shows Reply / Edit / Delete links the backend would reject;
  - deleting a reply no longer leaves a "ghost" entry in the thread.

nodes.title: Pure package value can't be mutated from another realm — "readonly tainted object"
nodes.data_json:
{"assignees":[],"body":"## Descriptions

> **Purpose of issue.** We are hitting a `cannot directly modify readonly tainted object`
> panic when a realm calls a pure math helper that performs in-place `uint256` arithmetic.
> We want to confirm **whether this is intended behavior** under interrealm v2's PkgID model.
> If it _is_ intended, our follow-up question is whether moving GnoSwap's math/arithmetic
> logic out of an `/r/` realm into a `/p/` pure package is the correct direction.

Under interrealm v2, a value of a type declared in a pure (`/p/`) package — e.g. `uint256.Uint` — can no longer be mutated in place when the mutation runs in a realm other than the one that owns the value's PkgID stamp.
The write is rejected at runtime with `cannot directly modify readonly tainted object`.

In GnoSwap this surfaces on the **core AMM math path**: any `/r/` realm that calls the tick/price helpers in `gno.land/r/gnoswap/common` (which internally do in-place `uint256` arithmetic such as `Lsh`/`Mul`) panics.
The minimal reproduction is a single line — an unrelated realm (`/r/onbloc/bar`) calling `common.TickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(...)` — so it is independent of pool, store, proxy, or mock code.

We measured (instrumented build) that the rejected object is stamped with the `/p/gnoswap/uint256` PkgID while the executing realm is `/r/gnoswap/common`, and `IsReadonlyBy` rejects the write purely on that PkgID mismatch.
This appears to contradict the documented rule that `/p/`-typed values are owned (and mutable) by the realm that **constructed** them.
We want to know whether this is intended; if so, whether relocating GnoSwap's math into a `/p/` package is the right fix.

---

### 1. Environment

- gno revision: `4166be993` — *"feat(gnovm): type-driven PkgID stamping for `*StructValue`at allocation & realm`.seal` (#5706)"\*
- `gno version`: `master.3098+4166be993`
- All `gnomod.toml` are `gno = "0.9"`.
- GnoSwap contracts revision: [`32d603b1`](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/commit/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860) (branch `tmp/refactor/interrealm-v2`) for migration.

### 2. Minimal reproduction

The whole failure reduces to **one line**: an arbitrary realm calling a pure `uint256` math helper.
No proxy, no store, no mock, no concentrated-liquidity code is involved.

**`gno.land/r/onbloc/bar` (a realm)** — calls a math helper that does in-place `uint256` arithmetic ([bar.gno#L66-L68](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/r/gnoswap/test_token/bar/bar.gno#L66-L68)):

```go
// PKGPATH: gno.land/r/onbloc/bar
import (
	u256 "gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256"
	"gno.land/r/gnoswap/common"
)

func CommonTickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(cur realm) {
	println(common.TickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(u256.MustFromDecimal("4295128740")))
}
```

**Test** ([bar_test.gno#L7-L9](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/r/gnoswap/test_token/bar/bar_test.gno#L7-L9)):

```go
// PKGPATH: gno.land/r/onbloc/bar
func TestCommonTickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(cur realm, t *testing.T) {
	CommonTickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(cur)
}
```

**The math helper** lives in `gno.land/r/gnoswap/common` (a realm package) ([tick_math.gno#L219-L235](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/r/gnoswap/common/tick_math.gno#L219-L235)):

```go
// PKGPATH: gno.land/r/gnoswap/common
func TickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(sqrtPriceX96 *u256.Uint) int32 {
	...
	// Scale ratio by 32 bits to convert from Q64.96 to Q96.128
	ratio := u256.Zero().Lsh(sqrtPriceX96, 32)   // <-- panics here
	...
}
```

`u256.Uint` is a `/p/`-declared named array type ([uint256.gno#L26](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/p/gnoswap/uint256/uint256.gno#L26), [uint256.gno#L43](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/p/gnoswap/uint256/uint256.gno#L43), [bitwise.gno#L63-L100](https://github.com/gnoswap-labs/gnoswap/blob/32d603b1f3aaa7539da3e8a46d3d7c6f2183d860/contract/p/gnoswap/uint256/bitwise.gno#L63-L100)):

```go
// PKGPATH: gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256
type Uint [4]uint64

func Zero() *Uint { return &Uint{0, 0, 0, 0} }

func (z *Uint) lsh(x *Uint, n uint) *Uint {
	...
	z[0] = z[0] << n   // <-- in-place write rejected as readonly tainted
	...
}
```

#### Result

```
panic: cannot directly modify readonly tainted object (use a method or crossing function): *(z)[0]
    gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256/bitwise.gno:100   (z[0] = z[0] << n)
    gno.land/r/gnoswap/common/tick_math.gno:235  (u256.Zero().Lsh(...))
    gno.land/r/onbloc/bar/bar.gno:67             (common.TickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio(...))
    gno.land/r/onbloc/bar/bar_test.gno:8
```

### 3. What we measured (instrumented build)

We patched `Machine.PopAsPointer2` (the `IndexExpr` write path) to print the realm and object PkgID at the moment `ro = m.IsReadonly(xv)` returns `true`:

```
DIAG IndexExpr ro=true:
  m.Realm.Path = gno.land/r/gnoswap/common
  m.Realm.ID   = RID057463ED46ACF77C2AF4CF7502764B767FC0DE87   (= gno.land/r/gnoswap/common)
  xv.PkgID     = RID4D3F9852A5F246086F70E5595208721EBD96ABCF   (= gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256)
  xv.IsZero    = false   (object is finalized/persisted, not a fresh allocation)
```

PkgID cross-check via `gnolang.PkgIDFromPkgPath`:

| Package                      | PkgID        | `IsImmutablePkg()` | `IsRealmPkg()` |
| ---------------------------- | ------------ | ------------------ | -------------- |
| `gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256` | `RID4D3F...` | **true**           | **false**      |
| `gno.land/r/gnoswap/common`  | `RID0574...` | false              | true           |

So at the rejected write:

- the executing realm is `gno.land/r/gnoswap/common`,
- the object being mutated is stamped `gno.land/p/gnoswap/uint256` (a `/p/`, `IsRealmPkg()==false` package),
- `IsReadonlyBy` returns `true` purely because `tvoid.PkgID != m.Realm.ID` (`ownership.go`):

  ```go
  // tv is an object residing in external realm
  if tvoid.PkgID != rid {
      return true
  }
  ```

### 4. Why this seems to contradict the documented model

The v2 docs/ADR state that a `/p/`-declared value's authority is the realm that **constructed** it, and that construction stamps the **active storage-context**:

- `docs/resources/gno-interrealm-v2.md` §3.2 — _"Every object's `ObjectID.PkgID` is set to the active realm-storage-context at the moment the allocator constructed it … the realm that holds authority over an object is the same realm that allocated it."_
- `gnovm/adr/interrealm_v2.md` (Borrow rules) — _"/p/-declared types used as values acquire a /r/ stamp (on ObjectInfo.PkgID) at construction and are then mutable by that /r/."_ and _"Statements like `foo.Bar = x` declared in /r/ … can only mutate /r/ types declared in the same realm, **or /p/ types constructed by the same realm.**"_

Under those rules, `u256.Zero()` allocated **inside** the `common` realm context should be stamped with `common`'s PkgID, and the subsequent in-place `Lsh` mutation (running with `m.Realm == common`) should be a same-realm write → no taint.

What we observe instead is that the mutated object is stamped with the **`/p/` type's own package PkgID (`uint256`)**, not the constructing realm (`common`).
That mismatch is what triggers the readonly taint.

Note that `stampPkgID` (`gnovm/pkg/gnolang/alloc.go`) is _supposed_ to fall back to `currentRealmID` for non-realm types:

```go
func (alloc *Allocator) stampPkgID(oi *ObjectInfo, t Type) {
	if pid := getDeclaredPkgID(t); pid.IsRealmPkg() { // uint256 is /p/ → IsRealmPkg()==false
		oi.SetPkgID(pid)
		return
	}
	oi.SetPkgID(alloc.currentRealmID)                 // expected path: stamp `common`
}
```

Since `uint256.IsRealmPkg() == false`, we would expect the object to be stamped `currentRealmID` (= `common`).
But the live object carries the `uint256` PkgID and is **finalized** (`IsZero()==false`), so it appears to acquire the `/p/` type's PkgID through some path, and to survive as persisted state.

### 5. Cross-check (isolation)

These pass, which localizes the problem to the realm-boundary crossing, not the arithmetic itself:

- `p/gnoswap/uint256` own tests: **PASS** (u256 allocated & mutated in the same `/p/` realmless context).
- `r/gnoswap/common` own tests calling `TickMathGetSqrtRatioAtTick` directly: **PASS** (same-realm).
- In `r/onbloc/bar`, `u256.Zero().Lsh(x, 32)` **inlined directly in bar**: **PASS**.
- In `r/onbloc/bar`, the same call **routed through `common.TickMathGetTickAtSqrtRatio`**: **FAIL (taint)**.

The only difference between the last two is whether the temporary `u256` value is allocated inside `bar`'s frame or inside `common`'s frame (reached via borrow rule #1, `m.Realm` shifts to `common` for the `/r/`-declared `common` function).

### 6. Questions for the gno team

1. **Is this intended?** Should a `/p/`-declared value type (`uint256.Uint`), allocated and mutated entirely within a single executing realm (`common`), be rejected as a `readonly tainted object` — given that §3.2 and the ADR say `/p/` types are "mutable by the realm that constructed them"?

2. If the object is expected to carry the **constructing realm's** PkgID (`common`), why does the live object carry the **`/p/` type's** PkgID (`uint256`) and appear finalized? Is `4166be993`'s type-driven stamping intended to apply to `/p/`-declared types, or only to `/r/`-declared types (as the commit message states: _"stamp … with the type's declared realm **when /r/-typed**"_)?

3. **If this is intended behavior**, then a `/r/` realm cannot host pure math helpers that perform in-place `/p/`-type arithmetic on values that originate in (or are borrowed into) a different realm context. In that case: is the correct remedy to move GnoSwap's arithmetic/math logic (e.g. `common`'s `tick_math`, `liquidity_amounts`) out of the `/r/gnoswap/common` realm and into a `/p/` pure package, so that no `/r/` borrow shift occurs and the temporary `uint256` values stay owned by the actual caller realm? Are there pitfalls with that approach we should know about (e.g. package-level `var` constants like `ratio0/ratio1`, `minSqrtRatio/maxSqrtRatio` that are constructed at `/p/` init)?

### 7. Pointers

- [`gnovm/adr/interrealm_v2.md`](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/master/gnovm/adr/interrealm_v2.md) — borrow rules, Foreign Type Value Caveat
- [`docs/resources/gno-interrealm-v2.md`](https://github.com/gnolang/gno/blob/master/docs/resources/gno-interrealm-v2.md) §3.2, §3.3, §4
